entries.basis: Anchored to the Billboard Hot 100 archive — every chart week since 1958 is browsable, and each pick here held the #1 spot.
entries.story: When [[The Beatles]] hit America in early 1964 they opened the door for a British Invasion, but the charts they stormed were shared ground: [[The Supremes]] and the Motown hit machine answered from Detroit, and by decade's end [[Aretha Franklin]], [[Marvin Gaye]], and [[Sly and the Family Stone]] had made soul the sound of American radio.

items.work: I Want to Hold Your Hand
I Heard It Through the Grapevine

entries.basis: Anchored to the Official Charts Company archive — every UK chart week since 1952 is browsable.
entries.story: Punk detonated in Britain while the singles chart stayed stubbornly mainstream: [[Sex Pistols]]' God Save the Queen was famously held at #2 in Jubilee week 1977 while [[Wings]]' Mull of Kintyre became the first UK single to sell two million. [[Donna Summer]]'s I Feel Love pointed at the dance future, and by 1979 punk's new-wave children — [[Ian Dury and the Blockheads]], [[Blondie]] — were topping the chart itself.

items.note: first UK single to sell two million, 1977

items.work: Hit Me with Your Rhythm Stick

entries.era: Ska, rocksteady, and the birth of reggae
entries.basis: Jamaica's radio charts from this era barely survive — this snapshot draws on documented sound-system and studio history.
entries.story: Jamaica's hits were made in the dance, not on a sales chart: Kingston sound systems run by producers like Coxsone Dodd and Duke Reid decided what the island moved to. [[Derrick Morgan]] and [[Prince Buster]] ruled the ska years, [[The Wailers]]' Simmer Down announced a new generation, and when the beat slowed in 1966, [[Alton Ellis]] and [[Hopeton Lewis]] made rocksteady the sound of Kingston. By 1968 [[Toots and the Maytals]] had given the next thing its name — reggae — and deejays like [[U-Roy]] turned the sound system itself into an instrument.

entries.era: Jovem Guarda and the festival era
entries.basis: No national chart existed — but the televised festival placements are real, documented rankings, and Jovem Guarda's dominance is television history.
entries.story: Brazil had no national chart, but it had something better documented: televised song festivals whose rankings the whole country argued about. [[Elis Regina]] won the first in 1965 with Arrastão, [[Chico Buarque]] shared the 1966 crown with A Banda, and in 1967 [[Caetano Veloso]] and [[Gilberto Gil]] detonated Tropicália from the festival stage — to boos as much as cheers. The biggest audience of all belonged to [[Roberto Carlos]] and the Jovem Guarda TV show, Brazil's own beat-pop craze.

items.work: Quero que vá tudo pro inferno

sources.label: Memórias da Ditadura — a era dos festivais

entries.basis: Nigeria's first real chart launched in 2020 — nothing from this era was charted. This snapshot leans on documented scene history and compilation liner scholarship (Strut's Nigeria 70, Soundway's Nigeria Special).
entries.story: Nigeria in the 1970s had no chart at all — popularity lived in Lagos clubs, on radio, and in record sales only anecdote recorded. [[Fela Kuti]] and Africa 70, powered by drummer [[Tony Allen]], made Afrobeat the country's political lightning rod, while the everyday commercial giants were the juju bandleaders [[King Sunny Adé]] and [[Ebenezer Obey]], trading album releases by the dozen. And the decade's biggest single came from highlife: [[Prince Nico Mbarga]]'s Sweet Mother, one of the best-selling African records ever made.

items.note: one of Africa's best-selling singles ever, 1976

sources.label: Red Bull Music Academy — King Sunny Adé

entries.basis: France had no official chart before November 1984 — this snapshot follows Salut les copains-era documentation; pre-1984 chart positions are later reconstructions.
entries.story: France's arbiter wasn't a chart — official charts only began in 1984 — it was Salut les copains, the radio show and million-selling magazine that crowned the yé-yé generation. [[Johnny Hallyday]] and [[Sylvie Vartan]] were its royal couple, [[Françoise Hardy]] turned Tous les garçons et les filles into the bedroom anthem of a generation, and [[France Gall]] took yé-yé to Eurovision victory in 1965. When Salut les copains threw a free concert at Place de la Nation in 1963, some 150,000 kids showed up.

items.work: Tous les garçons et les filles
La plus belle pour aller danser
Poupée de cire, poupée de son

entries.era: Schlager, Anglo pop — and a krautrock underground
entries.basis: Anchored to the German number-one archives; the split between what charted and what was later canonized is documented music history.
entries.story: West Germany's charts told an honest, unglamorous truth: the country bought schlager and international pop. [[Vicky Leandros]] and [[Heino]] ruled the German-language side while [[George Harrison]] and [[ABBA]] took the #1 spots from abroad. The krautrock now canonized worldwide — [[Can]], [[Neu!]], [[Faust]] — barely touched the domestic charts at the time; the great exception was [[Kraftwerk]], whose Autobahn drove into the German top ten and changed pop's future.

entries.basis: No sales chart existed — Binaca Geetmala's radio countdown (1952–1994) is the documented arbiter; its annual lists survive as reconstructions, so exact ranks are soft while the songs themselves are solid.
entries.story: India's popularity record wasn't a sales chart — it was Binaca Geetmala, the Radio Ceylon countdown the whole subcontinent tuned into for four decades. The playback singers were the real stars behind the screen idols: [[Lata Mangeshkar]] and [[Mohammed Rafi]] dominated the countdown's golden years, [[Mukesh]] gave Raj Kapoor his voice, and after the film Aradhana in 1969, [[Kishore Kumar]] became the defining male voice of the new decade.

entries.era: La Movida and the pop boom
entries.basis: Spain's official sales chart only starts in 1986 — this snapshot draws on documented Movida history and the Los 40 Principales airplay era.
entries.story: After Franco, Madrid exploded. La Movida Madrileña turned the city into an all-night laboratory: [[Alaska]] fronting Pegamoides and then Dinarama, [[Radio Futura]], and [[Nacha Pop]] — whose Chica de ayer became the movement's signature song. The honest footnote: Spain's best-selling pop of the decade belonged to [[Mecano]] and [[Hombres G]], the chart-friendly edge of the same generation.

sources.label: Rolling Stone en Español — La Movida

entries.basis: The USSR had no sales chart — Melodiya held a state monopoly on records. This snapshot presents the documented two-tier reality: official estrada on television, rock spread by hand-copied cassette.
entries.story: Soviet music ran on two levels. Above ground were the state-approved estrada stars and VIA ensembles: [[Alla Pugacheva]], the USSR's biggest performer, and bands like [[Zemlyane]], whose cosmonaut anthem Trava u doma every Soviet citizen could hum. Underground, the Leningrad Rock Club generation — [[Kino]], [[Akvarium]], [[Alisa]] — spread on hand-copied cassettes called magnitizdat, until glasnost let [[Viktor Tsoi]]'s call for change fill stadiums.

entries.basis: Anchored to the Billboard Hot 100 archive — the streaming-era chart, browsable week by week.
entries.story: By the mid-2010s the Hot 100 counted streams, and America's chart moved at internet speed. [[Mark Ronson]] and [[Bruno Mars]] opened the era with retro-funk, [[Adele]]'s Hello proved the event single still worked, [[Drake]] made streaming dominance routine, [[Luis Fonsi]] and [[Daddy Yankee]]'s Despacito held #1 for sixteen weeks — and [[Lil Nas X]]'s Old Town Road, a country-rap single born as a meme, outlasted them all with the longest #1 run in the chart's history.

items.note: with Bruno Mars — Hot 100 #1 for 14 weeks, 2015
with Daddy Yankee & Justin Bieber — Hot 100 #1 for 16 weeks, 2017

entries.basis: Anchored to the Official Charts Company archive; the UK chart has counted streams alongside sales since 2014.
entries.story: Britain's chart started counting streams in 2014, and the late 2010s belonged to the artists who mastered the new mathematics: [[Ed Sheeran]]'s Shape of You logged fourteen weeks at #1 in 2017, [[Drake]]'s One Dance managed fifteen the year before, and [[Adele]]'s Hello proved the old-fashioned event single still worked. By decade's end, homegrown voices closed the era — [[Stormzy]] took UK rap to #1 with Vossi Bop, and [[Lewis Capaldi]]'s Someone You Loved held on for seven weeks.

entries.era: Boleros, rancheras, and the mambo craze
entries.basis: Anchored to the reconstructed weekly charts of Selecciones Musicales magazine (sales, jukeboxes, airplay, and sheet music), aggregated by chart historian Fernando Mejía Barquera.
entries.story: Mexico's golden decade ran on XEW radio, and its weekly charts survive through the pages of Selecciones Musicales magazine. [[Pedro Infante]], the ranchera film idol, put Pobre corazón and Cien años at #1; [[José Alfredo Jiménez]] wrote the decade's songbook, sung by [[Jorge Negrete]] and [[Miguel Aceves Mejía]]; trios like [[Los Panchos]] made the bolero the language of romance across Latin America; and [[Pérez Prado]], Cuban-born and Mexico City-based, set the whole world dancing mambo.

items.note: his signature song, a top-ten staple of the early 1950s

entries.basis: Anchored to the Los 40 Principales countdown — an airplay chart launched in July 1966; Spain had no official sales chart in this era. Number ones per the archived weekly lists.
entries.story: Late-Franco Spain had a real pop scoreboard: Los 40 Principales, the radio countdown launched in July 1966. [[Raphael]] and [[Julio Iglesias]] rose through it, [[Massiel]] won Eurovision 1968 with La, la, la — after the regime refused to let [[Joan Manuel Serrat]] sing it in Catalan — and [[Nino Bravo]] and [[Fórmula V]] owned the early-seventies summers. Beneath the countdown ran a censored counter-current: more than 4,300 songs were banned from radio and TV under Franco, while nova cançó singers like [[Raimon]] made Catalan song itself an act of defiance.

items.note: voted among the greatest Spanish-language albums, 1971

entries.era: Psychedelic cumbia and the Amazon sound
entries.basis: Peru had no national chart in this era — its industry body only began charting in the 2000s. This snapshot draws on documented scene history and the compilation scholarship (The Roots of Chicha) that later carried the music worldwide.
entries.story: Peru rewired cumbia through the electric guitar. In Lima, [[Los Destellos]] — founded by guitarist [[Enrique Delgado]] — cut the records that made Peruvian cumbia a genre of its own, while out of the Amazon boom towns came [[Juaneco y Su Combo]], who lost five members, including guitarist Noé Fachín, in a 1977 plane crash, and [[Los Mirlos]], whose jungle-guitar sound became a style in itself. At home this was working-class dance music, long looked down on; abroad, decades later, the 2007 Roots of Chicha compilation made it a cult sound and sent Peru back to re-hear its own past.

items.note: the track that opens The Roots of Chicha

sources.label: Barbès Records — The Roots of Chicha

entries.era: Chicha — the sound of the migrant city
entries.basis: No chart existed — chicha's dominance is documented in scene history and press: the most widespread urban music of 1980s Peru, made by and for Andean migrants in Lima.
entries.story: As Andean migrants filled Lima, their music fused huayno with electric cumbia into chicha — dismissed by the middle class, inescapable everywhere else. [[Chacalón]] y La Nueva Crema sang directly to the new arrivals, and Soy provinciano became their anthem; [[Los Shapis]] carried chicha to national stardom in rainbow costumes. When Chacalón died in 1994, Peruvian press counted tens of thousands at his funeral — one of the largest Lima has ever seen.

items.note: breakthrough hit, adapted from a traditional huayno, 1981
